runContext.design_guide:
MLAI — Brand & Codebase Design Guide — React Router Article Surfaces Version: 2.0 (current codebase adaptation) — Scope: branding, tokens, article component usage, import paths, routing, accessibility, and one-shot generation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form article content that compiles first try in the current MLAI codebase. Applies to: article content under `app/articles/content/**`, article support components under `app/components/articles/**`, marketing/page headers, and reusable resource/CTA blocks. --- ## 1) Current framework and architecture ### 1.1 Framework This repository is a **React Router v7 + React 19 + Vite + TypeScript** app. Use: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` Do **not** use: ```tsx import Link from 'next/link' import Image from 'next/image' ``` Do **not** use: - `use client` - `use server` - `export const metadata` - `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - Next.js App Router conventions - Vue/Svelte syntax ### 1.2 Article architecture Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` Article content files should export a default React component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Critical article rules: - **Do not import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`**. The route handler/layout layer already handles article layout concerns. - Do not add duplicate article title/header/breadcrumb/author sections if the route/layout renders them. - Body content should return a fragment (`<>...`) or a neutral wrapper. - If using a wrapper, use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`. - Use existing article design-system components instead of raw ad-hoc card/callout/image markup. - Do not use placeholder image services such as `placehold.co`. - Use real image URLs, `ARTICLE_FALLBACK_IMAGE` where appropriate inside supported components, or explicit placeholders like `` only in templates. - Ensure registry slug path and SEO config path match exactly. --- ## 2) Brand identity ### 2.1 Palette and observed codebase colours The codebase uses a mix of MLAI token variables and bold brutalist article colours. | Token / colour | Value / usage | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | observed in components | Bright teal surface/accent. Used in `ArticleStepList` bullets and brand borders. | | `--brand-ink` | observed in links | Teal link/hover colour on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | observed in components | Charcoal/dark text on bright brand surfaces. | | `--soft` | observed in components | Soft teal panel background. | | `#ff3d00` | orange | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#4b1bd1` | purple | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#ffe900` | yellow | Brutalist yellow surface with black text. | | `#2b0f70` | deep purple | Resource CTA background. | | `#0d9488` | cyan/teal | Resource CTA background. | | `#1028E0` | blue | Existing `MLAITemplateResourceCTA` colour only. Do not invent new blue variants. | ### 2.2 Colour guardrails Use these existing patterns: - Purple surfaces: `bg-[#4b1bd1] text-white` - Orange surfaces: `bg-[#ff3d00] text-white` - Yellow surfaces: `bg-[#ffe900] text-black` - Transparent article panels: `bg-transparent border border-gray-300` or `border-gray-400` - Link emphasis on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline` - Neutral article text: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600` Do not: - Put `text-white` on yellow. - Use `bg-white` for generated article wrappers. - Invent new colour variants for existing components. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` for design-system article components that do not support those variants. - Overuse bright colours in long reading sections. --- ## 3) Typography, spacing, and shape The codebase uses Tailwind utility classes and `@tailwindcss/typography`. Common article surface shapes: ```tsx rounded-[22px] rounded-[28px] rounded-[32px] rounded-2xl rounded-3xl ``` Common spacing: ```tsx my-8 my-12 mt-8 mt-10 mt-12 p-6 p-7 p-8 sm:p-7 md:p-9 ``` Common text classes: ```tsx text-gray-900 text-gray-800 text-gray-700 text-gray-600 text-sm text-base text-lg text-xl text-2xl text-3xl sm:text-4xl font-bold ``` --- ## 4) Styling system ### 4.1 Tailwind setup The current Tailwind config is minimal and uses the typography plugin: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography'; import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss'; export default { content: ["./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [ typography, ], } satisfies Config; ``` Use Tailwind classes directly in TSX. Do not introduce CSS modules or styled-components. ### 4.2 Import convention Prefer the configured alias: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Use relative imports only when matching an existing nearby file pattern is clearly necessary. --- ## 5) Routing and links Internal links use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles Read more ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx External source ``` Do not use `href` on React Router `Link`. Use `to`. --- ## 6) Article component rules Use the existing article design-system components below. Do not create raw card grids, raw quote boxes, or raw image blocks when a component exists. ### 6.1 Persona / audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. Import: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Do not hand-code persona cards with raw `
` border/shadow markup. --- ### 6.2 Quotes and callouts Use `QuoteBlock` for strong callouts and quotes. Import: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'purple' | 'orange' ``` Example: ```tsx AI adoption works best when the team starts with a clear workflow, a measurable outcome, and a review loop for quality and risk. ``` Do not use non-existent variants such as: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`, not raw HTML image markup. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Props: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleImageBlockProps = { src: string alt: string width?: number height?: number containerClassName?: string imageClassName?: string caption?: string } ``` Do not use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.4 FAQ Use `ArticleFAQ`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` Example: ```tsx const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'How should a small team start with AI?', answer: 'Start with one workflow, define the outcome, and review outputs before scaling.', }, { question: 'Should every AI task be automated?', answer: ( <> Not necessarily. High-impact or high-risk decisions should keep a human review step, especially where accuracy, privacy, or customer trust matters. ), }, ] ``` `ArticleFAQ` renders its own heading. Do not add another duplicate FAQ heading immediately before it. --- ### 6.5 Step lists Use `ArticleStepList`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'teal' | 'brand' | 'indigo' ``` For generated content, prefer: ```tsx ``` Although `brand` exists in this component, prefer `teal` for one-shot generated articles unless matching an existing file. --- ### 6.6 Table of contents placeholder Use `ArticleTocPlaceholder` only when the article enhancer/layout is expected to populate it. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Optional no-margin use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.7 References Use `ArticleReferences`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` Supported reference categories: ```tsx 'government' | 'guide' | 'analysis' | 'industry' | 'watchlist' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.8 Resource lists Use `ArticleResourceList`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource, } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` Supported badge tones map to the internal badge variants: ```tsx 'red' | 'purple' | 'black' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx const resources: ArticleResource[] = [ { label: 'AI adoption checklist', description: 'A practical checklist for scoping, testing, and reviewing AI use cases.', href: '/articles', ctaLabel: 'Open', type: { label: 'Guide', tone: 'yellow' }, status: { label: 'Free', tone: 'purple' }, }, ] ``` Do not reference missing components: ```tsx ``` These are not available in the current context. --- ### 6.9 Article CTA blocks #### Simple company CTA Import: ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Props: ```tsx ``` Keep CTA text generic. Do not hard-code specific product names unless instructed. #### Resource CTA Import: ```tsx import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'purple' | 'cyan' | 'orange' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.10 Related article cards Use `ArticleCardBySlug` when linking to another registered article. Import: ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'default' | 'compact' ``` The component resolves registered article metadata and route slugs internally. --- ### 6.11 Footer article nav Use `ArticleFooterNav` for end-of-article navigation if the article pattern requires it. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Do not add if the route/layout already provides equivalent footer navigation. --- ### 6.12 Author bio Use `AuthorBio` only if the article pattern requires an in-body author section and the layout does not already render one. Import: ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Avoid duplicate author blocks. --- ## 7) Page header components ### 7.1 Article body files Most article content files should not manually render a hero/header. The route/layout handles article framing. Do not add: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' ``` Do not wrap with: ```tsx ... ``` ### 7.2 Standalone page hero For non-article standalone pages, `HeroSection` exists. Import: ```tsx import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'yellow' | 'beige' | 'white' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` For generated article content, avoid using this unless the task explicitly asks for a full standalone page. --- ## 8) Component imports — approved quick reference Use these exact imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Only import what the file uses. --- ## 9) Article file one-shot skeleton Use this structure for a new article content module: ```tsx import type { ReactNode } from 'react' import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the best first step?', answer: 'Start with a narrow workflow, a clear success measure, and a human review process.', }, ] const references = [ { id: 1, href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', title: 'Australian Government AI information', publisher: 'Department of Industry, Science and Resources', category: 'government', }, ] export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

Start with a concise introduction that explains the practical problem, who the guide is for, and what the reader will be able to do next.

Start with the workflow

AI decisions are easier when the team starts from the workflow instead of the tool. Map the current process, identify the bottleneck, and decide what a useful improvement would look like.

Treat AI as a workflow capability, not a magic feature. The useful question is: what decision, draft, search, summary, or review step can become clearer or faster?

Further reading

) } ``` Remove unused imports before final output. --- ## 10) Metadata, registry, and SEO consistency When adding or updating an article: - Content file path should follow: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` - Route slug should match the registry entry. - SEO config path should match the registry slug path exactly. - Internal article links should use the final public route: ```tsx ... ``` - Use kebab-case for filenames and slugs. - Use Australian English. - Date-sensitive claims should be qualified, for example: “as at 2026”. Do not invent registry or SEO export names. Follow the existing structures in: ```txt app/articles/registry app/articles/seo-config ``` --- ## 11) Accessibility checklist Apply to every generated UI/content change: - [ ] Headings follow semantic order. - [ ] Do not duplicate layout-rendered article titles or author sections. - [ ] Link text is descriptive. - [ ] External links use `target="_blank"` and `rel="noreferrer"`. - [ ] Images use meaningful `alt` text unless decorative. - [ ] No placeholder image URLs. - [ ] Colour contrast is appropriate: - yellow uses black text - purple/orange use white text - light/transparent panels use gray/black text - [ ] Interactive elements have visible focus styles. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Lists use semantic `
    `, `
      `, and `
    1. ` markup. --- ## 12) Copy and content rules Use the MLAI tone: - Australian English. - Practical, clear, and educational. - Define acronyms once. - Avoid hype, guarantees, and exaggerated claims. - Avoid implying official endorsement by government, universities, vendors, accelerators, or investors unless explicitly true. - When discussing AI, explain limits, review needs, data/privacy concerns, and verification steps in plain language. - When discussing startups or funding, keep the content educational and avoid financial advice language. - Do not add medical disclaimers. - Do not add domain-specific service chips or “browse services by need” tag groups. - Keep CTA text generic, for example: - “Get recommendations” - “Explore resources” - “Read the guide” - “Plan your next step” --- ## 13) Patterns and recipes ### 13.1 Internal article cross-link ```tsx how to get started with AI ``` ### 13.2 External source link ```tsx Australian Government AI resources ``` ### 13.3 Persona section ```tsx ``` ### 13.4 Strong callout ```tsx If the workflow requires judgement, accountability, or customer trust, keep a human review step. ``` ### 13.5 Image block ```tsx ``` ### 13.6 Step-by-step section ```tsx ``` ### 13.7 References section ```tsx ``` --- ## 14) Do / Don’t quick list Do: - Use `react-router` `Link` with `to`. - Use alias imports such as `~/components/articles/QuoteBlock`. - Use `AudienceGrid` for persona cards. - Use `QuoteBlock` for quotes/callouts. - Use `ArticleImageBlock` for article images. - Use `ArticleFAQ` for FAQs. - Use `ArticleReferences` for source lists. - Use `bg-transparent` for generated wrappers. - Keep article files as body content only. - Remove unused imports. Don’t: - Don’t import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Don’t use Next.js APIs or imports. - Don’t use raw `` for article images. - Don’t use `placehold.co`. - Don’t use raw bordered card grids for persona cards. - Don’t use duplicate article headers, breadcrumbs, author bios, or FAQ headings. - Don’t use unsupported component variants such as `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"`. - Don’t use `bg-white` in generated wrappers. - Don’t reference missing components such as `ArticleResourceDownload` or `ArticleSummaryCard`. - Don’t add medical disclaimers or service-chip sections. --- ## 15) Build and validation notes This project uses Bun-first scripts and React Router typegen/build commands. Relevant scripts: ```txt bun run build bun run typecheck bun run check:article-internal-links ``` Before finalising generated code: - Confirm all imports exist. - Confirm every imported component is used. - Confirm every component prop matches the actual TypeScript type. - Confirm no Next.js imports or directives appear. - Confirm article route path, registry slug, and SEO config path are consistent. - Confirm JSX returns a single fragment or valid wrapper. - Confirm no unsupported variants are used.
runContext.company_context:
# Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is a not-for-profit AI and machine learning community based in Australia. It brings together builders, learners, founders, researchers, students, clinicians, data scientists, AI developers, entrepreneurs, and industry supporters through events, hackathons, educational programs, community tools, and startup support initiatives.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial intelligence, machine learning, startup ecosystem, technology education/community  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation with events, educational programs, hackathons, and software-based founder/community tools  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia; broader relevance to “Australia and beyond” where AI community impact is discussed

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun, and impactful while fostering a vibrant Australian community passionate about AI and machine learning.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs and build a thriving AI ecosystem through meetups, hackathons, educational programs, practical community support, and meaningful professional connections.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, founders, students, clinicians, and practitioners.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world challenges, useful projects, and applied AI rather than abstract hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise members who help, volunteer, mentor, support founders, and share updates.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together people from different backgrounds — developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, healthcare professionals, students, sponsors, and mentors.
- **Transparency and momentum:** Encourage founders to build trust through consistent updates, clear asks, and visible progress.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use MLAI as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI and machine learning learners:** Students, early-career professionals, and curious beginners who want to understand AI, attend events, meet peers, and build confidence.
- **Builders and practitioners:** Data scientists, AI developers, software engineers, researchers, and technical contributors looking for practical challenges, hackathons, workshops, community support, and collaboration.
- **Startup founders and entrepreneurs:** Founders using AI or building technology companies who need pitch support, investor update habits, community feedback, introductions, and fundraising preparation.
- **Healthcare and medtech innovators:** Clinicians, doctors, nurses, pharmacists, healthcare professionals, students, data scientists, and AI builders interested in solving real healthcare bottlenecks through events such as MedHack.
- **Sponsors and ecosystem partners:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel intimidating, abstract, or hard to start with.
- Builders need real-world problems, collaborators, mentors, and structured opportunities to apply their skills.
- Founders often start raising money too late and do not build investor trust early enough.
- Early-stage founders struggle to communicate momentum, metrics, asks, risks, and updates clearly.
- Healthcare systems face bottlenecks around ageing populations, inpatient wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural care, and preventative medicine.
- Community members want simple ways to get involved, contribute meaningfully, and be recognised for helping.
- Sponsors need credible, values-aligned ways to support and reach the Australian AI ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, investors, founders, and technical peers.
- Build prototypes and solve real-world AI challenges through hackathons.
- Improve pitch clarity and communicate ideas effectively.
- Build investor relationships months before a raise.
- Turn community contribution into tangible perks such as coworking, events, mentoring, merch, and office hours.
- Support Australia’s AI and ML ecosystem through sponsorship, partnerships, and community involvement.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of MLAI’s offering as a community ecosystem made up of events, tools, hackathons, and contribution loops.

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   Meetups, workshops, social events, lightning talks, and educational gatherings across Australia for builders, learners, founders, and practitioners.

2. **Hackathons:**  
   Practical AI challenge events, including upcoming hackathons such as MedHack: Frontiers and the eSafety Hackathon. These events bring participants together to prototype solutions to real-world problems.

3. **MedHack: Frontiers:**  
   A healthcare AI hackathon focused on applying AI to pressing healthcare challenges. It brings clinicians, builders, data scientists, AI developers, mentors, judges, investors, and students together to prototype solutions across streams such as ageing populations, wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural healthcare, and preventative medicine.

4. **MLAI Vibe Raising:**  
   A founder tool that helps startups draft monthly investor updates from information the founder chooses to provide or optionally connect. It is designed to help founders build investor relationships before they raise by turning wins, asks, challenges, metrics, risks, and follow-ups into visible momentum.

5. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions and can be used for rewards such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, mentoring, merch, and other community perks.

6. **Founder education and resources:**  
   Guides and content such as “How to Pitch Your Big Idea” that help founders communicate complex ideas, use frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution, and close with clear calls to action.

7. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   Sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first, not just content-first:** MLAI is positioned around active participation, contribution, Slack involvement, volunteering, events, and practical support.
- **Practical AI focus:** Events and hackathons are tied to real-world challenges, not just theory or hype.
- **Cross-disciplinary collaboration:** MLAI brings together technical people, founders, students, researchers, clinicians, mentors, investors, and sponsors.
- **Founder support integrated into an AI community:** MLAI supports not only AI learning and building, but also startup pitching, investor updates, fundraising readiness, and founder visibility.
- **Contribution recognition through Roo Points:** MLAI has an internal system for recognising meaningful community contributions and turning them into practical perks.
- **Australian ecosystem focus:** The brand is explicitly focused on supporting Australia’s AI and ML community and making Australia a stronger home for AI entrepreneurship.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals — pick 1–2 per article, max:**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit community based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members” from diverse backgrounds including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals, and students.
- MLAI events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong community demand.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks, meetups, workshops, hackathons, and educational programs.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run in collaboration with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers uses Monash University Clayton Campus as a venue.
- MLAI positions itself as supporting Australia’s AI future and connecting sponsors with developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.
- MLAI Vibe Raising has privacy and terms pages covering Google OAuth, Gmail data use, connected sources, AI-assisted monthly founder updates, retention, deletion, and Limited Use commitments.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do not claim MLAI is a for-profit SaaS company; it is MLAI Aus Inc and is described as not-for-profit/non-profit.
- Do not claim Roo Points are money, wages, an exchange rate, or a guaranteed pathway to paid work. The site explicitly says they are internal community reward points with no cash value.
- Do not claim Vibe Raising provides financial, legal, tax, investment, medical, or professional advice.
- Do not claim AI-generated outputs from MLAI tools are always accurate; the terms state outputs may be inaccurate, incomplete, outdated, or misleading and must be reviewed.
- Do not claim MLAI sells personal information, Gmail data, Google user data, or connected-source data; the privacy policy says it does not.
- Do not imply Gmail or connected-data access is mandatory for Vibe Raising; connectors are optional.
- Do not invent sponsor names, award wins, funding numbers, partner lists, or member statistics beyond “over 10,000 members.”
- Do not claim participation in events, Roo Points, or volunteering guarantees jobs, team roles, event ownership, investor introductions, funding, or paid work.
- Do not overstate geographic reach beyond the stated Australian focus, though the site does mention “Australia and beyond” in relation to AI impact.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line

Choose one per article:

- “MLAI is a not-for-profit Australian community helping people learn, build, and connect around AI and machine learning.”
- “MLAI brings Australia’s AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, and partners together through events, hackathons, tools, and community programs.”
- “MLAI is an Australian AI and machine learning community focused on practical learning, real-world projects, and meaningful professional connections.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools, educational resources, and community contribution programs.”

### Primary CTAs

Use ONE per article, aligned to topic.

**For beginners/learners:**
> “Join an upcoming MLAI event to meet Australia’s AI and ML community and start learning with other builders.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Explore MLAI hackathons and build practical AI solutions with other developers, researchers, founders, and domain experts.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or relevant hackathon page such as `/medhack`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “Learn more about MedHack: Frontiers and how clinicians, builders, and AI practitioners are tackling real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “Use MLAI Vibe Raising to draft monthly investor updates and build investor relationships before you raise.”  
> Target: `/vibe-raising`

**For community members:**
> “Meet Roo, MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Connect with MLAI’s Australian AI community through events, hackathons, and educational programs.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`, depending on intent

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### URL Patterns or Pages to Link To

- Events: `https://mlai.au/events`
- Hackathons: `https://mlai.au/hackathons`
- MedHack: `https://mlai.au/medhack`
- Vibe Raising: `https://mlai.au/vibe-raising`
- Roo: `https://mlai.au/roo`
- Sponsors: `https://mlai.au/sponsors`
- Privacy policy for Vibe Raising / Google and Gmail data use: `https://mlai.au/privacy`
- Terms of service for Vibe Raising and connected-source use: `https://mlai.au/terms`
- Pitching guide: `https://mlai.au/how-to-pitch-your-idea`

### CTA Rules

- Use ONE CTA per article, either as a natural aside near the relevant section or as a final helpful next step.
- Match the CTA to the article’s topic and reader persona.
- Keep CTAs practical and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- For founder/fundraising articles, point to Vibe Raising or the pitching guide.
- For AI learning, community, and industry articles, point to events.
- For healthcare AI articles, point to MedHack.
- For sponsorship, ecosystem, or partnership articles, point to the sponsors page.
- For community participation and contribution articles, point to Roo.
- Mention privacy, optional connectors, and review requirements when discussing Vibe Raising in any data-sensitive context.

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use hard-sell language like “Buy now,” “Guarantee funding,” “Secure investors instantly,” or “Get paid through Roo Points.”
- Do not imply MLAI provides regulated professional advice.
- Do not push Vibe Raising as an automated replacement for founder judgement, investor relations, accounting, legal review, or financial advice.
- Do not direct every article to Vibe Raising; many MLAI readers are learners, builders, sponsors, or community members rather than founders.
- Do not promise event availability, acceptance, perks, mentoring, coworking, or rewards if capacity is limited.
- Do not imply joining Slack, attending an event, or earning Roo Points guarantees employment, sponsorship, investment, or leadership roles.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:**  
Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, accessible, optimistic, and slightly informal. MLAI’s tone is energetic and helpful without being overly corporate. It should feel like a capable community organiser speaking to founders, builders, learners, and partners.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over theoretical:** Focus on what people can build, learn, attend, draft, improve, or contribute.
- **Community-first:** Use language that invites participation and recognises contribution.
- **Accessible and encouraging:** Make AI feel approachable, especially for beginners and cross-disciplinary audiences.
- **Optimistic but grounded:** Celebrate AI’s potential while staying focused on real-world problems and responsible use.
- **Founder-aware:** For startup content, emphasise momentum, transparency, clear asks, investor trust, and starting early.
- **Technically accurate but not exclusionary:** Explain concepts clearly without dumbing them down or turning articles into jargon walls.

### Do’s

- Use plain-English explanations before technical depth.
- Use Australian context where relevant, especially for AI careers, events, community, startups, healthcare, and policy.
- Use community language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “members,” “mentors,” “contributors,” and “AI community.”
- Highlight practical formats: meetups, workshops, lightning talks, hackathons, Slack, founder updates, pitch nights, coworking, mentoring.
- When discussing pitching, use direct, useful frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution and clear calls to action.
- When discussing Vibe Raising, mention that founders can optionally connect Gmail or other sources and must review AI-generated outputs.
- When discussing Roo Points, describe them as internal community recognition or thank-you points, not money.
- Use clear CTAs that invite readers to attend, join, build, contribute, draft, learn, or partner.

### Don’ts

- Don’t sound like a generic enterprise AI vendor.
- Don’t overhype AI as a magic solution.
- Don’t present MLAI as a university, accelerator, recruitment agency, investment fund, or consulting firm unless future materials explicitly say so.
- Don’t imply MLAI owns or operates Monash University, MYMI, Google, Slack, Xero, Linear, Notion, or other third-party services.
- Don’t write in a patronising tone toward beginners.
- Don’t frame community contribution as unpaid labour-for-wages; Roo Points are recognition and perks, not compensation.
- Don’t make guarantees around fundraising, jobs, investment, AI accuracy, hackathon outcomes, or event acceptance.
- Don’t use excessive corporate buzzwords such as “synergistic transformation,” “AI revolution platform,” or “end-to-end disruption engine.”

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred for general articles, given the Australian focus. Preserve official names and quoted website wording where needed.
- **Preferred terms:**  
  AI, machine learning, AI community, Australian AI community, builders, learners, founders, contributors, mentors, hackathons, meetups, workshops, lightning talks, Roo, Roo Points, Vibe Raising, monthly investor updates, founder updates, meaningful contribution, community perks.
- **Avoided terms:**  
  Guaranteed funding, guaranteed job, cash value, wage, exchange rate, investment advice, legal advice, medical advice, professional advice, fully automated investor relations, instant investor access, replacement for review, AI certainty.

### Content Rules

- Position MLAI as a not-for-profit Australian AI and machine learning community, not as a generic SaaS vendor.
- Keep the core message practical: learn, build, connect, contribute, and solve real problems.
- Use the “over 10,000 members” proof point sparingly and accurately.
- For founder articles, emphasise building investor relationships before raising, monthly updates, transparency, metrics, asks, risks, and momentum.
- For healthcare AI articles, connect content to real healthcare bottlenecks and cross-disciplinary collaboration between clinicians and builders.
- For Roo-related articles, explain that Roo lives in Slack and helps manage contribution recognition, rewards, coworking, requests, and admin flows.
- For Vibe Raising articles, state that connected data sources such as Gmail are optional and that users choose what to provide or connect.
- Always include the review caveat for AI-assisted outputs when relevant.
- Avoid implying that MLAI’s tools, events, or community programs guarantee outcomes.
- Do not invent details about sponsorship tiers, sponsors, awards, leadership, founding year, funding, or event attendance numbers.
- Keep CTAs light, useful, and aligned to the reader’s likely next step.

org_config_snapshot.design_guide:
MLAI — Brand & Codebase Design Guide — React Router Article Surfaces Version: 2.0 (current codebase adaptation) — Scope: branding, tokens, article component usage, import paths, routing, accessibility, and one-shot generation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form article content that compiles first try in the current MLAI codebase. Applies to: article content under `app/articles/content/**`, article support components under `app/components/articles/**`, marketing/page headers, and reusable resource/CTA blocks. --- ## 1) Current framework and architecture ### 1.1 Framework This repository is a **React Router v7 + React 19 + Vite + TypeScript** app. Use: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` Do **not** use: ```tsx import Link from 'next/link' import Image from 'next/image' ``` Do **not** use: - `use client` - `use server` - `export const metadata` - `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - Next.js App Router conventions - Vue/Svelte syntax ### 1.2 Article architecture Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` Article content files should export a default React component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Critical article rules: - **Do not import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`**. The route handler/layout layer already handles article layout concerns. - Do not add duplicate article title/header/breadcrumb/author sections if the route/layout renders them. - Body content should return a fragment (`<>...`) or a neutral wrapper. - If using a wrapper, use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`. - Use existing article design-system components instead of raw ad-hoc card/callout/image markup. - Do not use placeholder image services such as `placehold.co`. - Use real image URLs, `ARTICLE_FALLBACK_IMAGE` where appropriate inside supported components, or explicit placeholders like `` only in templates. - Ensure registry slug path and SEO config path match exactly. --- ## 2) Brand identity ### 2.1 Palette and observed codebase colours The codebase uses a mix of MLAI token variables and bold brutalist article colours. | Token / colour | Value / usage | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | observed in components | Bright teal surface/accent. Used in `ArticleStepList` bullets and brand borders. | | `--brand-ink` | observed in links | Teal link/hover colour on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | observed in components | Charcoal/dark text on bright brand surfaces. | | `--soft` | observed in components | Soft teal panel background. | | `#ff3d00` | orange | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#4b1bd1` | purple | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#ffe900` | yellow | Brutalist yellow surface with black text. | | `#2b0f70` | deep purple | Resource CTA background. | | `#0d9488` | cyan/teal | Resource CTA background. | | `#1028E0` | blue | Existing `MLAITemplateResourceCTA` colour only. Do not invent new blue variants. | ### 2.2 Colour guardrails Use these existing patterns: - Purple surfaces: `bg-[#4b1bd1] text-white` - Orange surfaces: `bg-[#ff3d00] text-white` - Yellow surfaces: `bg-[#ffe900] text-black` - Transparent article panels: `bg-transparent border border-gray-300` or `border-gray-400` - Link emphasis on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline` - Neutral article text: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600` Do not: - Put `text-white` on yellow. - Use `bg-white` for generated article wrappers. - Invent new colour variants for existing components. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` for design-system article components that do not support those variants. - Overuse bright colours in long reading sections. --- ## 3) Typography, spacing, and shape The codebase uses Tailwind utility classes and `@tailwindcss/typography`. Common article surface shapes: ```tsx rounded-[22px] rounded-[28px] rounded-[32px] rounded-2xl rounded-3xl ``` Common spacing: ```tsx my-8 my-12 mt-8 mt-10 mt-12 p-6 p-7 p-8 sm:p-7 md:p-9 ``` Common text classes: ```tsx text-gray-900 text-gray-800 text-gray-700 text-gray-600 text-sm text-base text-lg text-xl text-2xl text-3xl sm:text-4xl font-bold ``` --- ## 4) Styling system ### 4.1 Tailwind setup The current Tailwind config is minimal and uses the typography plugin: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography'; import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss'; export default { content: ["./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [ typography, ], } satisfies Config; ``` Use Tailwind classes directly in TSX. Do not introduce CSS modules or styled-components. ### 4.2 Import convention Prefer the configured alias: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Use relative imports only when matching an existing nearby file pattern is clearly necessary. --- ## 5) Routing and links Internal links use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles Read more ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx External source ``` Do not use `href` on React Router `Link`. Use `to`. --- ## 6) Article component rules Use the existing article design-system components below. Do not create raw card grids, raw quote boxes, or raw image blocks when a component exists. ### 6.1 Persona / audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. Import: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Do not hand-code persona cards with raw `
` border/shadow markup. --- ### 6.2 Quotes and callouts Use `QuoteBlock` for strong callouts and quotes. Import: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'purple' | 'orange' ``` Example: ```tsx AI adoption works best when the team starts with a clear workflow, a measurable outcome, and a review loop for quality and risk. ``` Do not use non-existent variants such as: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`, not raw HTML image markup. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Props: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleImageBlockProps = { src: string alt: string width?: number height?: number containerClassName?: string imageClassName?: string caption?: string } ``` Do not use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.4 FAQ Use `ArticleFAQ`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` Example: ```tsx const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'How should a small team start with AI?', answer: 'Start with one workflow, define the outcome, and review outputs before scaling.', }, { question: 'Should every AI task be automated?', answer: ( <> Not necessarily. High-impact or high-risk decisions should keep a human review step, especially where accuracy, privacy, or customer trust matters. ), }, ] ``` `ArticleFAQ` renders its own heading. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'teal' | 'brand' | 'indigo' ``` For generated content, prefer: ```tsx ``` Although `brand` exists in this component, prefer `teal` for one-shot generated articles unless matching an existing file. --- ### 6.6 Table of contents placeholder Use `ArticleTocPlaceholder` only when the article enhancer/layout is expected to populate it. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource, } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` Supported badge tones map to the internal badge variants: ```tsx 'red' | 'purple' | 'black' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx const resources: ArticleResource[] = [ { label: 'AI adoption checklist', description: 'A practical checklist for scoping, testing, and reviewing AI use cases.', href: '/articles', ctaLabel: 'Open', type: { label: 'Guide', tone: 'yellow' }, status: { label: 'Free', tone: 'purple' }, }, ] ``` Do not reference missing components: ```tsx ``` These are not available in the current context. --- ### 6.9 Article CTA blocks #### Simple company CTA Import: ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Props: ```tsx ``` Keep CTA text generic. Do not hard-code specific product names unless instructed. #### Resource CTA Import: ```tsx import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'purple' | 'cyan' | 'orange' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.10 Related article cards Use `ArticleCardBySlug` when linking to another registered article. Import: ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'default' | 'compact' ``` The component resolves registered article metadata and route slugs internally. --- ### 6.11 Footer article nav Use `ArticleFooterNav` for end-of-article navigation if the article pattern requires it. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Do not add if the route/layout already provides equivalent footer navigation. --- ### 6.12 Author bio Use `AuthorBio` only if the article pattern requires an in-body author section and the layout does not already render one. Import: ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Avoid duplicate author blocks. --- ## 7) Page header components ### 7.1 Article body files Most article content files should not manually render a hero/header. The route/layout handles article framing. Do not add: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' ``` Do not wrap with: ```tsx ... ``` ### 7.2 Standalone page hero For non-article standalone pages, `HeroSection` exists. Import: ```tsx import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'yellow' | 'beige' | 'white' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` For generated article content, avoid using this unless the task explicitly asks for a full standalone page. --- ## 8) Component imports — approved quick reference Use these exact imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Only import what the file uses. --- ## 9) Article file one-shot skeleton Use this structure for a new article content module: ```tsx import type { ReactNode } from 'react' import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the best first step?', answer: 'Start with a narrow workflow, a clear success measure, and a human review process.', }, ] const references = [ { id: 1, href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', title: 'Australian Government AI information', publisher: 'Department of Industry, Science and Resources', category: 'government', }, ] export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

Start with a concise introduction that explains the practical problem, who the guide is for, and what the reader will be able to do next.

Start with the workflow

AI decisions are easier when the team starts from the workflow instead of the tool. Map the current process, identify the bottleneck, and decide what a useful improvement would look like.

Treat AI as a workflow capability, not a magic feature. The useful question is: what decision, draft, search, summary, or review step can become clearer or faster?

Further reading

) } ``` Remove unused imports before final output. --- ## 10) Metadata, registry, and SEO consistency When adding or updating an article: - Content file path should follow: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` - Route slug should match the registry entry. - SEO config path should match the registry slug path exactly. - Internal article links should use the final public route: ```tsx ... ``` - Use kebab-case for filenames and slugs. - Use Australian English. - Date-sensitive claims should be qualified, for example: “as at 2026”. Do not invent registry or SEO export names. Follow the existing structures in: ```txt app/articles/registry app/articles/seo-config ``` --- ## 11) Accessibility checklist Apply to every generated UI/content change: - [ ] Headings follow semantic order. - [ ] Do not duplicate layout-rendered article titles or author sections. - [ ] Link text is descriptive. - [ ] External links use `target="_blank"` and `rel="noreferrer"`. - [ ] Images use meaningful `alt` text unless decorative. - [ ] No placeholder image URLs. - [ ] Colour contrast is appropriate: - yellow uses black text - purple/orange use white text - light/transparent panels use gray/black text - [ ] Interactive elements have visible focus styles. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Lists use semantic `
    `, `
      `, and `
    1. ` markup. --- ## 12) Copy and content rules Use the MLAI tone: - Australian English. - Practical, clear, and educational. - Define acronyms once. - Avoid hype, guarantees, and exaggerated claims. - Avoid implying official endorsement by government, universities, vendors, accelerators, or investors unless explicitly true. - When discussing AI, explain limits, review needs, data/privacy concerns, and verification steps in plain language. - When discussing startups or funding, keep the content educational and avoid financial advice language. - Do not add medical disclaimers. - Do not add domain-specific service chips or “browse services by need” tag groups. - Keep CTA text generic, for example: - “Get recommendations” - “Explore resources” - “Read the guide” - “Plan your next step” --- ## 13) Patterns and recipes ### 13.1 Internal article cross-link ```tsx how to get started with AI ``` ### 13.2 External source link ```tsx Australian Government AI resources ``` ### 13.3 Persona section ```tsx ``` ### 13.4 Strong callout ```tsx If the workflow requires judgement, accountability, or customer trust, keep a human review step. ``` ### 13.5 Image block ```tsx ``` ### 13.6 Step-by-step section ```tsx ``` ### 13.7 References section ```tsx ``` --- ## 14) Do / Don’t quick list Do: - Use `react-router` `Link` with `to`. - Use alias imports such as `~/components/articles/QuoteBlock`. - Use `AudienceGrid` for persona cards. - Use `QuoteBlock` for quotes/callouts. - Use `ArticleImageBlock` for article images. - Use `ArticleFAQ` for FAQs. - Use `ArticleReferences` for source lists. - Use `bg-transparent` for generated wrappers. - Keep article files as body content only. - Remove unused imports. Don’t: - Don’t import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Don’t use Next.js APIs or imports. - Don’t use raw `` for article images. - Don’t use `placehold.co`. - Don’t use raw bordered card grids for persona cards. - Don’t use duplicate article headers, breadcrumbs, author bios, or FAQ headings. - Don’t use unsupported component variants such as `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"`. - Don’t use `bg-white` in generated wrappers. - Don’t reference missing components such as `ArticleResourceDownload` or `ArticleSummaryCard`. - Don’t add medical disclaimers or service-chip sections. --- ## 15) Build and validation notes This project uses Bun-first scripts and React Router typegen/build commands. Relevant scripts: ```txt bun run build bun run typecheck bun run check:article-internal-links ``` Before finalising generated code: - Confirm all imports exist. - Confirm every imported component is used. - Confirm every component prop matches the actual TypeScript type. - Confirm no Next.js imports or directives appear. - Confirm article route path, registry slug, and SEO config path are consistent. - Confirm JSX returns a single fragment or valid wrapper. - Confirm no unsupported variants are used.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI -- One-Shot React Router Article/Resource Generation Spec (TSX) -- v2025-12-15 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent as a "system" / "developer" prompt so the agent can generate MLAI article/resource content for the current `mlai-au` codebase in one shot. The output must compile in the React Router v7 + React 19 + TypeScript + Tailwind project without manual corrections. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Resources should be practical, inclusive, factual, Australian-context aware, and date-qualified where helpful. Avoid hype, guarantees, exaggerated claims, and unsupported statistics. Output format: `.tsx` article content module(s) for the existing React Router codebase. Framework: React Router v7, React 19.2.1, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS. Package/runtime: Bun-first project. Build/typecheck commands use `bun run typecheck` and `bun run build`. Do not generate a PDF. Do not use ReportLab. Do not use Next.js patterns. 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given a Title, Slug, Category, Description, Date, optional Hero Image, article body outline/sections, FAQs, references, and optional CTA requirements, generate a TypeScript React article content file that adheres 100% to the rules below. Primary file path: `app/articles/content/{category-slug}/{article-slug}.tsx` The file must: - Use `.tsx` - Export article metadata constants when needed by existing registry patterns - Default export a function component: `export default function ArticlePage() { ... }` - Return article body content only - NOT import or wrap in `ArticleLayout` - NOT render a duplicate article hero/title/header if the route/layout already renders it - Use React Router links from `react-router` - Use actual codebase components from `~/components/articles/...` - Use Tailwind utility classes and project CSS variables already present in the codebase - Compile with TypeScript 3-viewer/browser QA: article renders in local dev, production build, and deployed React Router route without hydration errors, broken imports, invalid props, duplicated headers, or missing registry/SEO path consistency. Deliverables - `app/articles/content/{category-slug}/{article-slug}.tsx` -- primary article content module - Registry/SEO updates only if the existing repository pattern requires them: - `app/articles/registry.ts` - `app/articles/seo-config.ts` - Do not invent new shared components or unsupported props. 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: ```ts { topic: "{{TOPIC}}", title: string, slug: string, categorySlug: string, description: string, datePublished: string, // ISO date, e.g. "2026-01-15" heroImage?: string, // use a real URL or "", never placehold.co heroImageAlt?: string, sections: Array<{ heading: string, body: string | JSXContentPlan, subsections?: Array<{ heading: string, body: string | JSXContentPlan }> }>, audienceCards?: Array<{ title: string, description: string, variant: "orange" | "purple" | "yellow" }>, quoteBlocks?: Array<{ title?: string, variant: "purple" | "orange", body: string }>, images?: Array<{ src: string, alt: string, caption?: string }>, faqs?: Array<{ question: string, answer: string | JSXContentPlan }>, references?: Array<{ href: string, title: string, publisher: string, description?: string, category?: "government" | "guide" | "analysis" | "industry" | "watchlist" }>, cta?: { title: string, body: string, buttonText: string, buttonHref: string, note?: string } } ``` Example input schema for planning only: ```ts { topic: "{{TOPIC}}", title: "How to learn AI in Australia", slug: "how-to-learn-ai-in-australia", categorySlug: "ai-learning-resources", description: "A practical guide to learning AI in Australia, including community, projects, and credible resources.", datePublished: "2026-01-15", heroImage: "", heroImageAlt: "People learning artificial intelligence together", audienceCards: [ { title: "Students", description: "People exploring AI, machine learning, and data skills for study or early career decisions.", variant: "orange" }, { title: "Career switchers", description: "Professionals who want a practical path into AI without starting from scratch.", variant: "purple" }, { title: "Founders and builders", description: "Startup-minded learners who want to apply AI to real products and workflows.", variant: "yellow" } ], cta: { title: "Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}", body: "Explore practical MLAI resources and community pathways related to this topic.", buttonText: "Browse related resources", buttonHref: "/articles" } } ``` 2) Framework, routing, and project configuration Framework detection is authoritative: - Framework: React Router v7 - Rendering library: React 19.2.1 - Language: TypeScript - Component extension: `.tsx` - Styling: Tailwind CSS + existing CSS variables - Build tool: Vite - Router imports: `react-router` - Link component: `...` Never use: - `next/link` - `next/image` - `next/dynamic` - `next/font` - `'use client'` - `'use server'` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - `generateStaticParams` - `export const metadata` - Vue or Svelte syntax - CSS modules or styled-components unless already present in the touched file Use these project conventions: ```tsx import type { ReactNode } from 'react' import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Import ordering: 1. React/type imports 2. React Router imports 3. Icon imports, if any 4. Internal component imports 5. Article utilities/authors, if used No semicolons in article content files. 3) File path and export rules Primary article content file: ```txt app/articles/content/{categorySlug}/{slug}.tsx ``` Example: ```txt app/articles/content/ai-learning-resources/how-to-learn-ai-in-australia.tsx ``` Required component export: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Content files must NOT do this: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' export default function ArticlePage() { return ( ... ) } ``` `ArticleLayout` is handled by the route/registry layer. Importing or wrapping with `ArticleLayout` in the content file is a build/design failure. Metadata constants may be used when consistent with nearby article files: ```tsx const TOPIC = '{{TOPIC}}' const TITLE = '...' const DESCRIPTION = '...' const SLUG = '...' const CATEGORY = '...' const DATE_PUBLISHED = '2026-01-15' const HERO_IMAGE = '' ``` Do not invent a new metadata API. If updating `app/articles/registry.ts` or `app/articles/seo-config.ts`, follow the exact existing object shape in those files. Path consistency is mandatory: - Registry slug/path must match the content file slug. - SEO config path must match the registry route path exactly. - Example route path: `/articles/{slug}` - Example content path: `app/articles/content/{categorySlug}/{slug}.tsx` 4) Header, title, hero, and layout rules No duplicate headers. ), }, ] const references = [ { id: 1, href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', title: 'Artificial intelligence', publisher: 'Australian Government Department of Industry, Science and Resources', description: 'Australian Government information about AI policy, adoption, and guidance.', category: 'government', }, { id: 2, href: 'https://www.csiro.au/', title: 'CSIRO artificial intelligence research', publisher: 'CSIRO', description: 'Research and information from Australia’s national science agency.', category: 'analysis', }, ] export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

{TOPIC} can feel broad at first, but it becomes easier to understand when you connect it to practical problems, local communities, and small projects you can actually complete.

This guide gives you a grounded way to approach the topic in Australia, with a focus on learning by doing and using credible sources.

Start with a practical question

The fastest way to make progress is to choose a specific question. For example, instead of asking how to learn everything about AI, ask what kind of AI system you want to understand, evaluate, build, or use.

Treat {TOPIC} as something to practise, not just something to read about. A small project, a clear question, and a feedback loop will teach you more than a passive list of resources.

Use credible Australian context

Australian learners and founders should pay attention to local policy, privacy expectations, community events, and industry examples. Official and reputable sources can help you separate durable concepts from short-term hype.

For broader context, start with Australian Government resources and reputable institutions such as{' '} CSIRO . You can also explore related MLAI articles in the{' '} article library .

Build a feedback loop

A useful feedback loop includes a project, a way to test your understanding, and a community or peer group that can challenge your assumptions. This helps you improve faster and avoid learning only in theory.
